n_estimators: 300
max_depth: 7
colsample_bytree: 0.8
colsample_bylevel: 0.8
learning_rate: 0.03
reg_lambda: 2
subsample: 0.9

Model-4 : Performance on train, validation¶

In [55]:
# compute performance metrics on training data set
model4_tuned_train_perf = model_performance_regression(model4_tuned, X_train, y_train)

# compute performance metrics on validation data
model4_tuned_val_perf = model_performance_regression(model4_tuned, X_val, y_val)

# print and compare model performances for train and validation data
model4_performance = model_performances(model4_tuned_train_perf, model4_tuned_val_perf)
model4_performance
Out[55]:
RMSE MAE R-squared Adj. R-squared MAPE
Training 0 194.947124 79.537925 0.966706 0.966652 0.029356
Validation 0 283.135407 114.107511 0.927252 0.926782 0.043176

Model-4 performance analysis¶

  • Overall fit:
    • Validation R-squared = 0.927 (strong) but lower than tuned Random Forest (≈0.931)
    • no lift over tuned Random Forest.
  • Generalization:
    • Validation RMSE 283.1 is higher (worse) than tuned Random Forest (274.9) and close to baseline Random Forest (≈283)
    • Tuned XGB did not improve error.
  • Variance indication:
    • Train RMSE 194 vs Validation 283 (ratio ≈0.68) shows a larger gap than tuned Random Forest (≈0.79).
    • This implies comparatively higher variance / residual overfitting.
  • Relative error:
    • Validation MAPE 4.31% higher than tuned Random Forest (~4.0%). Proportional accuracy has slightly degraded.
  • Bias–variance trade:
    • Lower train error than tuned Random Forest (194 vs 219) but worse Validation error—suggests additional regularization
    • Fine-tuning further with lambda, max_depth or use early stopping could help.

Overall : Model-3 and Model-4¶

Conclusion: XGB Regressor tuned underperforms compared to RandomForest tuned

Model Performance Comparison, Final Model Selection, and Serialization¶

Training performance comparison¶

  • we will compare the performance of all models on the training dataset
In [56]:
# create a combined dataframe of training performances of all models
models_train_comp_df = pd.concat([
    model1_train_perf.T,
    model2_train_perf.T,
    model3_tuned_train_perf.T,
    model4_tuned_train_perf.T
    ],axis=1,
)

# Add column names corresponding to the models
models_train_comp_df.columns = [
    "Random Forest Regressor",
    "XGBoost Regressor",
    "Random Forest Regressor Tuned",
    "XGBoost Regressor Tuned"
]

# print the model performance on training data
print("Training performance comparison:")
models_train_comp_df

Training performance comparison:
Out[56]:
Random Forest Regressor XGBoost Regressor Random Forest Regressor Tuned XGBoost Regressor Tuned
RMSE 109.537658 117.273537 219.468736 194.947124
MAE 41.544960 58.852848 86.271635 79.537925
R-squared 0.989489 0.987951 0.957803 0.966706
Adj. R-squared 0.989472 0.987932 0.957735 0.966652
MAPE 0.015690 0.020035 0.032972 0.029356

Validation performance comparison¶

In [57]:
# create a combined dataframe of valing performances of all models
models_val_comp_df = pd.concat([
    model1_val_perf.T,
    model2_val_perf.T,
    model3_tuned_val_perf.T,
    model4_tuned_val_perf.T
    ],axis=1,
)

# Add column names corresponding to the models
models_val_comp_df.columns = [
    "Random Forest Regressor",
    "XGBoost Regressor",
    "Random Forest Regressor Tuned",
    "XGBoost Regressor Tuned"
]

# print the model performance on validation data
print("Validation performance comparison:")
models_val_comp_df

Validation performance comparison:
Out[57]:
Random Forest Regressor XGBoost Regressor Random Forest Regressor Tuned XGBoost Regressor Tuned
RMSE 283.551307 304.136128 274.960000 283.135407
MAE 107.590238 136.608427 107.749367 114.107511
R-squared 0.927038 0.916060 0.931393 0.927252
Adj. R-squared 0.926567 0.915518 0.930949 0.926782
MAPE 0.040346 0.050221 0.041200 0.043176

Best Model Selection¶

In [58]:
# print difference in model performance on train and validation data w.r.t RMSE
(models_train_comp_df - models_val_comp_df).iloc[0]
Out[58]:
Random Forest Regressor         -174.013648
XGBoost Regressor               -186.862591
Random Forest Regressor Tuned    -55.491264
XGBoost Regressor Tuned          -88.188283
Name: RMSE, dtype: float64

Observations:

  • Overfitting:
    • RMSE 'train to test' gaps (absolute values) indicate overfitting severity across all models with 'Tuned Random Forest Regressor' having the smallest gap of 55
    • Others have,
      • Baseline RandomForest ~=174
      • Baseline XGB ~=186 (highest)
      • Tuned XGB ~=88 (improved)
  • Generalization:
    • Smallest gap in Tuned Random Forest Regressor implies it has the best generalization among the 4 models
    • Variance in Tuned random forest substantially reduced compared to both baselines and tuned XGB.
  • Predictive Accuracy:
    • Tuned RandomForest also has the lowest test RMSE and highest test R-squared among the four (seen in earlier comparison tables), confirming superior predictive accuracy.
  • More:
    • Tuned XGB still shows a larger gap (≈88) and higher test error, offering no performance lift over tuned Random Forest.

Conclusion: '

  • Tuned Random Forest Regressor (Model 3)' selected as the best model for serialization and deployment
  • It has the best balance of accuracy and generalization among the 4 models.

Serialization : Best Model¶

Note: Based on the model comparison, the 'Random Forest Regressor-tuned model' demonstrated the best performance on the testing data. Therefore, we will serialize this model for deployment.

In [59]:
# Create a folder for storing the files needed for web app deployment. Serialized Model will be stored here
os.makedirs("backend_files", exist_ok=True)
In [60]:
# Define the file path to save (serialize) the trained model along with the data preprocessing steps
saved_model_path = "backend_files/superkart_sales_revenue_prediction_model_v1_0.joblib"
In [61]:
# Save the best model selected i.e., Random Forest Regressor Tuned model pipeline using joblib
joblib.dump(model3_tuned, saved_model_path)
print(f"Random Forest Regressor Tuned Model saved successfully at {saved_model_path}")

Random Forest Regressor Tuned Model saved successfully at backend_files/superkart_sales_revenue_prediction_model_v1_0.joblib

Load : Serialized Model¶

In [62]:
# Load the saved model pipeline from the file
saved_model = joblib.load("backend_files/superkart_sales_revenue_prediction_model_v1_0.joblib")
# Confirm the model is loaded
print("Random Forest Regressor Tuned Model loaded successfully.")

Random Forest Regressor Tuned Model loaded successfully.

Predict: Using saved Best model on test data¶

In [63]:
# compute performance metrics on test data using the saved model
saved_model_test_perf = model_performance_regression(saved_model, X_test, y_test)
# print model performance on test data
saved_model_test_perf
Out[63]:
RMSE MAE R-squared Adj. R-squared MAPE
0 278.492986 113.394723 0.932027 0.931676 0.041087
In [64]:
# print best model performance seen earlier on validation data
model3_tuned_val_perf
Out[64]:
RMSE MAE R-squared Adj. R-squared MAPE
0 274.96 107.749367 0.931393 0.930949 0.0412

Best Model Performance on unseen test data¶

Comparison (Test vs Validation):

  • RMSE: 278.49 vs 274.96 (↑ +3.53; ~+1.3%) – very small increase; expected sampling variation.
  • MAE: 113.39 vs 107.75 (↑ +5.64; ~+5.2%) – modest rise; slightly more larger individual errors on unseen data.
  • R-squared: 0.932 vs 0.931 (≈ +0.001) – essentially unchanged; variance explained is stable.
  • Adjusted R-squared: 0.931 vs 0.930 – consistent, no indication of overfitting via excess features.
  • MAPE: 0.040 vs 0.041 (slight improvement) – relative percentage accuracy maintained.

Interpretation:

  • Generalization is strong: only minor drift in absolute error metrics; no material overfitting.
  • Slight MAE increase suggests a few mid-range deviations; not systematic.
  • Stable (even marginally improved) R² + lower MAPE confirms robustness.

Conclusion:

  • Performance on unseen test data closely matches validation
  • Model is reliable for deployment
  • No immediate retraining needed

Deployment - Backend¶

Setting up a Hugging Face Docker Space for the Backend¶

  • Creation of 'Hugging Face Docker Space' for deploying the backend will be automated using the Hugging Face Hub API.

Login to Hugging Face¶

In [65]:
# Import the login function from the huggingface_hub library
from huggingface_hub import login

# Login to your Hugging Face account using your access token
# login will prompt the HF token. For security purpose not directly provided in codes
login()

# Import the create_repo function from the huggingface_hub library
from huggingface_hub import create_repo
In [66]:
# Check if logged in
# Import the whoami function from the huggingface_hub library
from huggingface_hub import whoami

# print the username if logged in
try:
    user_info = whoami()
    print(f"Logged in as: {user_info['name']}")
except:
    print("Not logged in")

Logged in as: nsriram78

Create the backend docker repo space¶

In [67]:
# Import the create_repo function from the huggingface_hub library
from huggingface_hub import create_repo

# Try to create the repository for the Hugging Face Space
try:
    create_repo("SuperKartRevenuePredictionBackend",  # backend space name
        repo_type="space",  # Repository type is "space"
        space_sdk="docker",  # Space SDK is "docker"
        private=False  # Creates as public space
    )
except Exception as e:
    # Handle potential errors during repository creation
    if "RepositoryAlreadyExistsError" in str(e):
        print("Repository already exists. Skipping creation.")
    else:
        print(f"Error creating repository: {e}")

Flask Web Framework¶

In [68]:
%%writefile backend_files/app.py
# Import necessary libraries
import joblib  # For loading the serialized model
import pandas as pd  # For data manipulation
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify  # For creating the Flask API
from datetime import datetime  # added for dynamic current year

# Initialize the Flask application
sales_revenue_predictor_api = Flask("SuperKart Sales Revenue Predictor")

# Load the trained machine learning model
model = joblib.load("superkart_sales_revenue_prediction_model_v1_0.joblib")

# Define a route for the home page (GET request)
@sales_revenue_predictor_api.get('/')
def home():
    """
    This function handles GET requests to the root URL ('/') of the API.
    It returns a simple welcome message.
    """
    return "Welcome to the SuperKart Product Sales Revenue Prediction API !"

# Define an endpoint for single product sales revenue prediction (POST request)
@sales_revenue_predictor_api.post('/v1/revenue')
def predict_sales_revenue():
    """
    This function handles POST requests to the '/v1/revenue' endpoint.
    It expects a JSON payload containing property details and returns
    the predicted product sales revenue as a JSON response.
    """
    # Get the JSON data from the request body
    property_data = request.get_json()

    # Extract relevant features from the JSON data
    sample = {
        'Product_Weight': property_data['Product_Weight'],
        'Product_Sugar_Content': property_data['Product_Sugar_Content'],
        'Product_Allocated_Area': property_data['Product_Allocated_Area'],
        'Product_Type': property_data['Product_Type'],
        'Product_MRP': property_data['Product_MRP'],
        'Store_Size': property_data['Store_Size'],
        'Store_Location_City_Type': property_data['Store_Location_City_Type'],
        'Store_Type': property_data['Store_Type'],
        'Store_Establishment_Year': property_data['Store_Establishment_Year']
    }

    # Convert the extracted data into a Pandas DataFrame
    record_input_data = pd.DataFrame([sample])

    # Compute Store_Age
    current_year_value = datetime.now().year
    record_input_data['Store_Age'] = current_year_value - record_input_data['Store_Establishment_Year']

    # Define bins and labels (open-ended last bin for 50+ years)
    age_bins = [0, 10, 20, 30, float("inf")]
    age_labels = ["<10 Years", "10–20 Years", "20–30 Years", "30+ Years"]

    # Create binned column
    record_input_data["Store_Age_Binned"] = pd.cut(
        record_input_data["Store_Age"],
        bins=age_bins,
        labels=age_labels,
        right=False
    )

    # Make prediction (get revenue)
    predicted_store_revenue = model.predict(record_input_data)[0]

    # Convert predicted_price to Python float
    predicted_store_revenue = round(float(predicted_store_revenue), 2)
    # When we send this value directly within a JSON response, Flask's jsonify function encounters a datatype error

    # Return the actual price
    return jsonify({'Predicted Product_Store_Sales_Total': predicted_store_revenue})


# Define an endpoint for batch prediction (POST request)
@sales_revenue_predictor_api.post('/v1/revenuebatch')
def predict_sales_revenue_batch():
    """
    This function handles POST requests to the '/v1/revenuebatch' endpoint.
    It expects a CSV file containing property details for multiple properties
    and returns the predicted product sales revenue as a dictionary in the JSON response.
    """
    # Get the uploaded CSV file from the request
    file = request.files['file']

    # Read the CSV file into a Pandas DataFrame
    csv_input_data = pd.read_csv(file)

    # Compute Store_Age
    current_year_value = datetime.now().year
    csv_input_data['Store_Age'] = current_year_value - csv_input_data['Store_Establishment_Year']
    # Define bins and labels (open-ended last bin for 50+ years)
    age_bins = [0, 10, 20, 30, float("inf")]
    age_labels = ["<10 Years", "10–20 Years", "20–30 Years", "30+ Years"]

    # Create binned column
    csv_input_data["Store_Age_Binned"] = pd.cut(
        csv_input_data["Store_Age"],
        bins=age_bins,
        labels=age_labels,
        right=False
    )

    # Make predictions for all properties in the DataFrame (get log_prices)
    predicted_store_revenues = model.predict(csv_input_data).tolist()

    # Create a dictionary of predictions with product IDs as keys
    if 'Product_Id' not in csv_input_data.columns:
        return jsonify({"error": "Input file must contain a 'Product_Id' column"}), 400

    # Create a dictionary of predictions with Product IDs as keys
    product_ids = csv_input_data['Product_Id'].tolist()  # Assuming 'Product_Id' is the property ID column
    output_dict = dict(zip(product_ids, predicted_store_revenues))  # Use actual prices

    # Return the predictions dictionary as a JSON response
    return jsonify(output_dict)

# Run the Flask application in debug mode if this script is executed directly
if __name__ == '__main__':
    sales_revenue_predictor_api.run(debug=True)

Writing backend_files/app.py

Dependencies File¶

In [69]:
%%writefile backend_files/requirements.txt
pandas==2.3.0
numpy==2.0.2
scikit-learn==1.6.1
xgboost==3.0.2
joblib==1.5.0
Werkzeug==3.1.3
flask==3.1.2
gunicorn==23.0.0
requests==2.32.3
uvicorn==0.34.2
streamlit==1.49.1

Writing backend_files/requirements.txt

Dockerfile¶

In [70]:
%%writefile backend_files/Dockerfile
FROM python:3.12-slim

# Set the working directory inside the container
WORKDIR /app

# Copy all files from the current directory to the container's working directory
COPY . .

# Install dependencies from the requirements file without using cache to reduce image size
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade -r requirements.txt

# Define the command to start the application using Gunicorn with 4 worker processes
# - `-w 4`: Uses 4 worker processes for handling requests
# - `-b 0.0.0.0:7860`: Binds the server to port 7860 on all network interfaces
# - `app:app`: Runs the Flask app (assuming `app.py` contains the Flask instance named `app`)
CMD ["gunicorn", "-w", "4", "-b", "0.0.0.0:7860", "app:sales_revenue_predictor_api"]

Writing backend_files/Dockerfile

Uploading Files to Hugging Face Space (Docker Space)¶

In [71]:
# for hugging face space authentication to upload files
from huggingface_hub import HfApi

repo_id = "nsriram78/SuperKartRevenuePredictionBackend"  # HuggingFace space id

# Initialize the API
api = HfApi()

# Upload Streamlit app files stored in the folder called backend_files
api.upload_folder(
    folder_path="backend_files",  # Local folder path
    repo_id=repo_id,  # Hugging face space id
    repo_type="space",  # Hugging face repo type "space"
)
Out[71]:
CommitInfo(commit_url='https://huggingface.co/spaces/nsriram78/SuperKartRevenuePredictionBackend/commit/2f7294aeb5f7b9811d748e9d9ee199ac90587ea3', commit_message='Upload folder using huggingface_hub', commit_description='', oid='2f7294aeb5f7b9811d748e9d9ee199ac90587ea3', pr_url=None, repo_url=RepoUrl('https://huggingface.co/spaces/nsriram78/SuperKartRevenuePredictionBackend', endpoint='https://huggingface.co', repo_type='space', repo_id='nsriram78/SuperKartRevenuePredictionBackend'), pr_revision=None, pr_num=None)

Testing Backend deployment (Flask API)¶

  • We will test the backend API separately as it is decoupled from the frontend and deployed separately
In [72]:
# Set the model root URL to the deployed backend space URL
model_root_url = "https://nsriram78-SuperKartRevenuePredictionBackend.hf.space/"  # Base URL of the deployed backend in hugging face

#copy the csv data to a dataframe
app_test_dataset = superkart_data.copy()

Test POST /v1/revenue API¶

In [73]:
# Complete URL for single prediction endpoint
single_prediction_url = model_root_url + "/v1/revenue"

# convert the first record in X_test to a JSON for sending as payload
payload = app_test_dataset.head(1).to_dict(orient="records")[0]

# print the payload
print(payload)

# Sending a POST request to the model endpoint with the test payload
response = requests.post(single_prediction_url, json=payload)

# Check if request was successful
if response.status_code == 200:
    print("\nResponse received : ")
    print(response.json())   # Print parsed JSON response
else:
    print(f"Error {response.status_code}: {response.text}")

{'Product_Id': 'FD6114', 'Product_Weight': 12.66, 'Product_Sugar_Content': 'Low Sugar', 'Product_Allocated_Area': 0.027, 'Product_Type': 'Frozen Foods', 'Product_MRP': 117.08, 'Store_Id': 'OUT004', 'Store_Establishment_Year': 2009, 'Store_Size': 'Medium', 'Store_Location_City_Type': 'Tier 2', 'Store_Type': 'Supermarket Type2', 'Product_Store_Sales_Total': 2842.4}

Response received : 
{'Predicted Product_Store_Sales_Total': 2889.13}

Observations:

  • We can observe the Single Record API working well
  • We can notice that the predicted value 2889.13 is close to the actual value of 2842.4

Test POST /v1/revenuebatch API¶

In [74]:
# Take only the first 25 records
batch_payload = app_test_dataset.head(25)

# Save to CSV
batch_payload.to_csv("batch_test.csv", index=False)

# Keep product IDs and actual sales for comparison
actual_revenues = batch_payload[['Product_Id', 'Product_Store_Sales_Total']].copy()

# Complete URL for batch prediction endpoint
batch_prediction_url = model_root_url + "/v1/revenuebatch"

# Send the CSV file to the batch API endpoint
with open("batch_test.csv", "rb") as f:
    response = requests.post(batch_prediction_url, files={"file": f})

# Check response
if response.status_code == 200:
    predictions = response.json()

    # Convert predictions dict to DataFrame
    pred_df = pd.DataFrame(list(predictions.items()), columns=['Product_Id', 'Predicted Product_Store_Sales_Total'])

    # Merge actual_revenues with predictions
    comparison = actual_revenues.merge(pred_df, on='Product_Id', how='left')

    # Print the comparison
    print(comparison.head(20))  # show first 20 rows for quick check

else:
    print(f"Error {response.status_code}: {response.text}")

   Product_Id  Product_Store_Sales_Total  Predicted Product_Store_Sales_Total
0      FD6114                    2842.40                          2889.127817
1      FD7839                    4830.02                          4926.985808
2      FD5075                    4130.16                          4081.480744
3      FD8233                    4132.18                          4048.350404
4      NC1180                    2279.36                          2114.877796
5      FD5680                    2629.15                          2637.742359
6      FD5484                    5081.14                          5074.953371
7      NC5885                    4494.62                          4464.796989
8      FD1961                    1684.82                          1696.676795
9      NC6657                    2531.30                          2611.398431
10     DR2699                    3385.46                          3383.085370
11     FD6027                    3123.19                          3107.600245
12     FD8553                    3660.82                          3726.467496
13     FD5276                    3635.50                          3671.430858
14     FD1231                    3587.27                          3561.843956
15     FD1387                    3776.76                          3719.698012
16     NC1071                    2839.53                          2803.757419
17     FD2625                    3328.99                          3331.700866
18     FD4982                    4347.91                          4393.450895
19     NC7289                    3529.29                          3565.205229

Observations:

  • We can observe the Batch API working well for the batch request of 20 records
  • Also the predicted values are close to the actual values

Hugging Face Backend Space Information¶

  1. Backend Space URL: https://huggingface.co/spaces/nsriram78/SuperKartRevenuePredictionBackend
  2. API endpoint URL
    • for single prediction: https://nsriram78-SuperKartRevenuePredictionBackend.hf.space/v1/revenue
    • for batch prediction: https://nsriram78-SuperKartRevenuePredictionBackend.hf.space/v1/revenuebatch
## Working backend app screenshot

Deployment - Frontend¶

Create the frontend docker repo space¶

In [75]:
# Create the frontend repository for the Hugging Face Space
try:
    create_repo("SuperKartRevenuePredictionFrontend",  # backend space name
        repo_type="space",  # Repository type is "space"
        space_sdk="docker",  # Space SDK is "docker"
        private=False  # Creates as public space
    )
except Exception as e:
    # Handle potential errors during repository creation
    if "RepositoryAlreadyExistsError" in str(e):
        print("Repository already exists. Skipping creation.")
    else:
        print(f"Error creating repository: {e}")

Create frontend_files directory¶

In [76]:
# Create a folder for storing the files needed for frontend UI deployment
os.makedirs("frontend_files", exist_ok=True)

# Create a folder for storing the streamlit config
os.makedirs("frontend_files/.streamlit", exist_ok=True)

Streamlit for Interactive UI¶

In [77]:
%%writefile frontend_files/app.py
import streamlit as st
import pandas as pd
import requests
from datetime import datetime

import os
# force config dir inside repo
os.environ["STREAMLIT_CONFIG_DIR"] = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), ".streamlit")

# --- Page Config ---
st.set_page_config(
    page_title="SuperKart Product Sales Revenue Predictor",
    page_icon="🛒",
    layout="centered",
)

# --- Title ---
st.title("🛒 SuperKart Product Sales Revenue Predictor")
st.markdown("Enter product and store details below to predict sales revenue, or upload a CSV for batch predictions.")

# --- Layout for Product + Store Info ---
st.header("📦 Product Information")
col1, col2 = st.columns(2)

with col1:
    product_weight = st.number_input("Product Weight", min_value=0.0, step=0.01)
    product_sugar_content = st.selectbox("Product Sugar Content", ["Low Sugar", "Regular", "No Sugar"])
    product_allocated_area = st.number_input("Product Allocated Area", min_value=0.0, step=0.001)

with col2:
    product_type = st.selectbox(
        "Product Type",
        ["Meat", "Snack Foods", "Hard Drinks", "Dairy", "Canned", "Soft Drinks",
         "Health and Hygiene", "Baking Goods", "Bread", "Breakfast", "Frozen Foods",
         "Fruits and Vegetables", "Household", "Seafood", "Starchy Foods", "Others"]
    )
    product_mrp = st.number_input("Product MRP", min_value=0.0, step=0.01)

st.header("🏬 Store Information")
col3, col4 = st.columns(2)

with col3:
    store_establishment_year = st.number_input(
        "Store Establishment Year", min_value=1980, max_value=datetime.now().year, step=1
    )
    store_size = st.selectbox("Store Size", ["Small", "Medium", "High"])

with col4:
    store_location_city_type = st.selectbox("Store Location City Type", ["Tier 1", "Tier 2", "Tier 3"])
    store_type = st.selectbox("Store Type", ["Departmental Store", "Supermarket Type1", "Supermarket Type2", "Food Mart"])

# --- Build Input Data ---
input_data = pd.DataFrame([{
    "Product_Weight": product_weight,
    "Product_Sugar_Content": product_sugar_content,
    "Product_Allocated_Area": product_allocated_area,
    "Product_Type": product_type,
    "Product_MRP": product_mrp,
    "Store_Establishment_Year": store_establishment_year,
    "Store_Size": store_size,
    "Store_Location_City_Type": store_location_city_type,
    "Store_Type": store_type
}])

# --- Prediction Button ---
st.markdown("---")
if st.button("🔮 Predict Sales Revenue"):
    response = requests.post(
        "https://nsriram78-SuperKartRevenuePredictionBackend.hf.space/v1/revenue",
        json=input_data.to_dict(orient="records")[0]
    )
    if response.status_code == 200:
        prediction = response.json()["Predicted Product_Store_Sales_Total"]
        st.metric(label="Predicted Product Sales Revenue", value=f"{prediction:,.2f}")
    else:
        st.error("⚠️ Error making prediction. Please try again.")

# --- Batch Prediction Section ---
st.markdown("---")
st.header("📂 Batch Prediction")

with st.form("batch_form"):
    uploaded_file = st.file_uploader("Upload CSV file for batch prediction", type=["csv"])
    submit_button = st.form_submit_button("🚀 Predict Batch")

if submit_button and uploaded_file is not None:
    response = requests.post(
        "https://nsriram78-SuperKartRevenuePredictionBackend.hf.space/v1/revenuebatch",
        files={"file": uploaded_file}
    )
    if response.status_code == 200:
        predictions = response.json()
        st.success("✅ Batch predictions completed!")
        st.dataframe(pd.DataFrame(list(predictions.items()), columns=["Product_Id", "Predicted Sales Revenue"]))
    else:
        st.error("⚠️ Error making batch prediction.")

Writing frontend_files/app.py

Dependencies File¶

In [78]:
%%writefile frontend_files/requirements.txt
pandas==2.3.0
requests==2.32.3
streamlit==1.49.1

Writing frontend_files/requirements.txt

DockerFile¶

In [79]:
%%writefile frontend_files/Dockerfile
# Use a minimal base image with Python 3.9 installed
FROM python:3.12-slim

# Set the working directory inside the container to /app
WORKDIR /app

# Copy all files from the current directory on the host to the container's /app directory
COPY . .

# Enable Streamlit to use a writable folder for its config (like the working directory):
ENV STREAMLIT_CONFIG_DIR="/home/user/app/.streamlit"

# Install Python dependencies listed in requirements.txt
RUN pip3 install -r requirements.txt

# Define the command to run the Streamlit app on port 8501 and make it accessible externally
CMD ["streamlit", "run", "app.py", "--server.port=7860", "--server.address=0.0.0.0", "--server.enableXsrfProtection=false"]

# NOTE: Disable XSRF protection for easier external access in order to make batch predictions

Writing frontend_files/Dockerfile

Add Streamlit config file¶

  • following config is added to avoid CORS issues when the frontend calls the backend API
  • also it is added to avoid the error due to .streamlit folder not being writable
In [80]:
%%writefile frontend_files/.streamlit/config.toml
[server]
headless = true
port = 7860
enableCORS = false

[browser]
gatherUsageStats = false

Writing frontend_files/.streamlit/config.toml

    repo_type="space",  # Hugging face repo type "space"
)

Hugging Face Frontend Space Information¶

Working front-end app screenshot¶

Single Record screenshot with predition results¶

Front-end App : Batch prediction screenshot¶

Actionable Insights and Business Recommendations¶

Key Highlights: Actionable Insights and Business Recommendations¶

  • Empowered Store Managers:
    • Predictions empower store managers to align procurement with expected demand, cutting down stockouts and reducing overstock holding costs.
    • Predictions not constrained to only provided Store Ids and Product Ids; can be used for new stores/products.
  • Optimization Planning
    • With batch forecasting enabled, regional planning teams can optimize product allocation across outlets, especially in Tier-2 and Supermarket Type2 stores (highest contributors).
  • Revenue drivers:
    • High store size, Departmental / Supermarket Type2 formats, Tier 1 locations, higher MRP, heavier products, older stores.
  • Underperformers:
    • Tier 3 locations, Food Mart format, Starchy Foods category, low‑MRP light items.
  • Best model deployed:
    • Tuned RandomForestRegressor (Test RMSE ≈ 278, R² ≈ 0.93, MAPE ≈ 4.0%).
    • The tuned RandomForest model predicts quarterly product–store sales revenue with high accuracy (R² ≈ 0.93, RMSE ≈ 278).
  • Deployment:
    • Decoupled Flask API + Streamlit UI (single & batch prediction) via Hugging Face Docker Spaces.

Detailed Actionable Insights & Recommendations¶

  • Product mix & assortment
    • Expand strong categories (Fruits & Vegetables, Household, Health & Hygiene) in Tier-1 and growing Tier-2 stores.
    • Reduce or bundle slow-moving items like Starchy Foods to clear space.
  • Pricing & promotions
    • Protect margins on premium/high-MRP products.
    • Review mid-tier prices quarterly to capture margin where demand is less sensitive.
    • Move promotions from sugar-based themes to value/category bundles (e.g., Household + Hygiene).
  • Store formats & inventory
    • Upgrade medium stores in Tier-1/Tier-2 to high format for growth.
    • Use weekly batch forecasts: increase safety stock for high-revenue SKUs, discount/mark down low performers.
  • Forecast reliability
    • Model’s stable accuracy (MAPE ≈ 4%) means forecasts are within ~140 error for an average sale, reliable for budgeting and procurement.
    • Watch SKUs with unusually high errors (>2× RMSE) and retrain if rolling 4-week RMSE rises >10%.
  • Integration & governance
    • Embed API scoring into procurement workflows.
    • Log all predictions and keep version history for transparency.

Model Limitations and Business Considerations¶

  • Seasonality & external shocks
    • Current model does not capture effects of festivals, inflation, or supply chain disruptions.
    • Future versions should integrate external/seasonal data for more resilient forecasts.
  • How to use forecasts
    • Predictions are most useful for strategic planning and resource allocation, not as exact revenue guarantees at store level.
    • RandomForest shows which features matter but not “why” sales change; a simple interpretation dashboard may be needed for business confidence.
    • While there’s low regulatory risk, teams should communicate forecast uncertainty to avoid blind reliance in automated replenishment.
  • Model maintenance
    • Retraining every quarter is important as SuperKart adds stores or products, to avoid accuracy drift.
    • Adding new product or store types requires a pipeline update (encoding changes).
  • Future improvements
    • For very low-volume products, percentage errors (MAPE) are less stable; use absolute error checks as well.
    • Tuned RandomForest works well now, but lacks explicit time patterns; consider a hybrid of time-series + ML in the next iteration.

Power Ahead!
